executiveSummary:
# Medicaid Program Executive Summary

National Medicaid enrollment totaled 74.9 million enrollees as of February 2026, with all major states experiencing enrollment declines as post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations continue. California leads with 12.4 million enrollees despite a significant 6.8% year-over-year decrease, while Michigan shows the steepest decline at 7.6%. Texas maintains 4.0 million enrollees with a moderate 3.8% decline, positioning it as the third-largest program nationally behind California and New York. Total program expenditures reached $216 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national average per-enrollee cost of $2,883. Notably, per-enrollee costs vary dramatically across states, ranging from Florida's efficient $2,036 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's $4,363, while Texas falls near the national average at $3,014 per enrollee.

Quality performance remains a critical concern, with the national average score at 65.6 out of 100 and only 10 states achieving high-performer status above 75 points. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a moderate score of 62, significantly trailing top performers like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), but avoiding the bottom tier occupied by Southern peers Mississippi (48) and Alabama (52). The state demonstrates strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, covering nearly 3.9 million enrollees. Current regulatory activity suggests continued federal oversight focus on provider enrollment moratoria and state-directed payment arrangements, which may impact program operations and financial planning in the coming fiscal year.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.0 million enrollees, demonstrating relatively stable enrollment dynamics compared to peer states. The state's -3.8% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns closely with similar large non-expansion states like Florida (-4.2%) and Georgia (-3.9%), while outperforming the steep declines seen in expansion states like Michigan (-7.6%) and Illinois (-5.7%). This suggests Texas is managing the post-pandemic unwinding process more effectively than many peers. At $3,014 per enrollee, Texas maintains spending levels slightly above the national average of $2,883, positioning it between the ultra-lean Florida model ($2,036) and higher-cost expansion states. The state's total expenditure of $12.0 billion represents efficient cost management for a program serving nearly 13% of the state's population.

Texas shows particular strength in managed care adoption, with 93.3% penetration significantly exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery. However, the state's quality performance remains a key area for improvement, scoring 62/100 and ranking 30th nationally—well below top performers like Massachusetts (81) but avoiding the bottom tier occupied by neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51). The concentration of recent federal regulatory activity around managed care state-directed payments and provider enrollment moratoria suggests heightened scrutiny of Texas's managed care operations. Moving forward, Texas should leverage its managed care leadership to drive quality improvements while maintaining its cost-effectiveness advantage, particularly as federal oversight intensifies around payment methodologies and network adequacy requirements.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 51% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,363 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,883, indicating potential cost efficiency concerns.

alerts.title: Florida Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,036 is 29% below the national average, representing notably efficient spending or potential service limitations.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Scores
alerts.description: Mississippi (48), Oklahoma (51), Alabama (52), South Carolina (53), and Arkansas (54) all scored below 55 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: High-Cost States Show Mixed Quality Performance
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines high per-enrollee spending ($4,221) with excellent quality scores (81), while Pennsylvania has similarly high spending ($4,363) but quality data is not available for comparison.

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining faster than key competitor states
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% YoY while NC held flat at 0.0% and PA only declined 2.9%. This suggests Texas may be implementing more aggressive redetermination processes.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Accelerated enrollment decline could impact contract volumes and revenues while creating operational pressures on eligibility systems that Accenture manages.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment trend analysis to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score ranks 30th nationally at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th out of 50 states and falling below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the moderate performer tier.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality performance could trigger federal oversight, impact federal funding, and create opportunities for quality improvement initiatives that Accenture could lead.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement opportunities with HHSC quality team

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration exceeds national average significantly
executiveInsights.summary: Texas managed care penetration at 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%. This represents 3.86M managed care enrollees out of 4.0M total.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates strong operational maturity but also means most efficiency gains have been captured, requiring focus on advanced optimization strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities beyond basic penetration

executiveInsights.title: New community engagement requirements proposed by CMS
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows proposed Medicaid community engagement requirements that could affect eligibility processes. This represents a significant policy shift requiring operational changes.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New federal requirements could necessitate major system modifications and process changes that Accenture would need to implement for HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag community engagement rule to HHSC policy team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs above national average but below peers
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $3,014 per enrollee compared to national average of $2,883, but significantly less than similar large states like NY ($3,460) and PA ($4,363).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Moderate spending levels suggest Texas has cost control but may indicate underinvestment in services or quality, creating opportunities for targeted improvements.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost efficiency benchmarking with HHSC finance team

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with a 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in performance-based contracts and CMS oversight.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting specific underperforming measures.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration creates significant contract renewal exposure with potential for major wins or losses.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Engage early with HHSC procurement team to position for upcoming managed care contract opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 3.8% YoY enrollment decline may trigger budget pressures and program restructuring affecting existing contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Model financial impact scenarios and prepare cost optimization proposals for HHSC leadership.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal regulatory shifts
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple CMS policy changes around state directed payments and provider enrollment create compliance requirements and modernization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively develop regulatory compliance solutions and position as trusted advisor for policy implementation.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight increases audit risk but also creates demand for compliance and fraud prevention services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and develop OIG-ready documentation standards.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $3,014 per-enrollee cost is above national average, creating pressure for cost containment initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop data analytics solutions to identify cost reduction opportunities while maintaining quality outcomes.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive procurement landscape
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas procurement portal signals new opportunities but increased competition from other consulting firms.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated Texas procurement monitoring process and strengthen local partnership network.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid has 3,997,752 enrollees as of February 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.8% year-over-year, consistent with national unwinding trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spent $3,014 per enrollee in fiscal year 2024, below the national average of $2,883.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas managed care penetration is 93.3%, significantly above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks 30th of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62, below the national average of 65.6.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two Texas-specific procurement portals are actively monitoring opportunities: HHSC and ESBD.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Eight active federal regulatory items affecting Medicaid programs are currently in progress.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates are available in the current data set.
